free_text: Neutron Spectral and Angular Distribution
Measurements for 113 and 256 MeV Protons on
range-thick Al and U-238 Targets Using the Foil
Activation Technique
Further
cross section adjustments.
IPNS - Intense Pulsed Neutron Source,
the first spallation slow neutron source based on a
proton synchrotron.
Foil activation technique consists
of activation of small packet of foil or wire
materials, measuring the activation products.
Whenever possible, the data an procedures are tested
in well-characterized neutron field with
time-of-flight technique. The irradiations were
performed in the beam dump area of the Intense
Pulsed Neutron Source at Argonne National Laboratory.
Following each irradiation, the aluminum beam foils
were autoradiographed by placing the 10 cm x 10 cm
square foils directly on top of polaroid film. Three
0.13 mm thick Al foils were irradiated in the proton
beam, the front and rear end foils were used to
correct for the recoil of activated atoms from the
central foil. The middle foil was then segmented and
gamma counted for 7Be, 22Na, and 24Na. The
irradiation times varied from 60 to 90 minutes.
STAY'SL least-squares computer code was used to
solve integral equations for the neutron flux spe
where the cross sections and integral values were
known to adjust neutron spectrum to give the best fit
to the data.
The flux uncertainties are less than
20% between 1 and 200 MeV.
Compiler comment: E-MAX values were added by a
compiler, there are no such data column in the
original article.
